name: The Near Future (aller + infinitive)
summary: Talk about what's going to happen with aller plus an infinitive, just like English 'going to'.
explanation:
The easiest way to talk about the future in French is the futur proche: conjugate aller (to go) in the present, then add an infinitive. Je vais manger (I am going to eat) works exactly like the English 'going to'.

First you need aller: je vais, tu vas, il va, nous allons, vous allez, ils vont. Then attach any infinitive: je vais partir (I'm going to leave), nous allons voir (we are going to see).

To make it negative, wrap aller in ne ... pas: je ne vais pas sortir (I'm not going to go out). This form is far more common in speech than the simple future tense.

examples.en: We are going to leave tomorrow.
You are going to like this.

examples.native: Je ne vais pas sortir ce soir.
examples.en: I'm not going to go out tonight.

commonMistakes: Conjugating the second verb. After aller it stays an infinitive: je vais partir, not je vais pars.
Forgetting to conjugate aller itself. It must agree with the subject: nous allons, not nous va.
Placing the infinitive before aller. The order is aller + infinitive.
Negating only one part: it is je ne vais pas manger.
Using the present of the main verb to mean the future. Use aller + infinitive instead.
tip: Think 'going to'. Conjugate aller, add the action, and you have a natural, everyday future.
